title: The A to Z of AI Music in 2025, Part 1: AI Technologies to Fair Use - Music Ally
text:
Music Ally has been writing about the intersection of AI and music for 11 years now: our first story was about
early AI-music startup Jukedeck winning a startups prize in December 2014
.
For several years it felt like a niche topic, but in 2025 it’s been
the
big story for the music industry. Except it’s not just one story: it’s a host of interwoven trends, deals, opportunities, anxieties, legal questions and controversies. Not to mention philosophical and often existential anxieties about AI’s impact on human musicians.
What could be more human than to try to make sense of it all using the alphabet? Here’s our look-back at the key talking points around AI and music in 2025, with a view to what might happen next in 2026.
A is for AI Technologies
This may seem like an obvious place to start, it’s the plural ‘technologies’ that’s important. AI isn’t one thing: it’s a group of technologies that work in different ways and have many different applications.
The vast majority of headlines around AI and music focus on generative-AI music creation and the controversies around it. Which are certainly important, but they aren’t the full story.
Other forms of AI are being used for music recommendations; making sense of analytics; separating stems; automating marketing tasks; creating visual assets; generating legal contracts; mixing and mastering; analysing catalogues of music and improving their metadata; and detecting fraud, for example.
These areas have their own controversies, particularly when the use of AI may displace humans. But it’s always useful to remember this bigger picture: AI is already having a significant impact on the music industry and on human musicians – sometimes for better and sometimes for worse – in a bunch of ways beyond just generating music.
B is for Bleeding Verse
Bleeding Verse is one of a number of GenAI-music projects that reached a sizeable audience on streaming services this year, showing that we are moving into a world where human musicians compete with GenAI for listeners perhaps faster than we thought.
This project made headlines in October as the subject of
a series of angry social posts by Lucas Woodland
, frontman of rock band Holding Absence – one of the acknowledged influences on Bleeding Verse’s creation. He noted that the latter’s 916,000 Spotify listeners were more than Holding Absence’s 853,000.
“It’s shocking, it’s disheartening, it’s insulting – most importantly – it’s a wake up call. Oppose AI music, or bands like us stop existing,” wrote Woodland. But Bleeding Verse’s creator
went on to sign a deal with Hallwood Media
– more on that company when we get to ‘H’ – and has grown its Spotify audience to more than 1.1 million listeners.
Note our use of the word ‘creator’ in that last sentence. There are humans behind projects like Bleeding Verse: writing lyrics and worldbuilding around the characters. It’s not just a case of pressing a button and a copycat song from a clone artist pops out. Even so, the anger and anxiety of the humans whose work was fed into the systems that generate the music is understandable – and should not be ignored.
C is for ChatGPT
OpenAI’s ChatGPT is still the first service many people will name if asked about AI tools. This year its growth continued,
reaching 800 million weekly active users by early October
– which is also when it was the focus for a significant music partnership.
The partner was Spotify, which had already been using tech from OpenAI to power its DJ feature. It followed that in October by launching an integration for people to connect their Spotify and ChatGPT accounts, giving them the ability to play music through the chatbot as well as tap it for recommendations.
Quickly hailed by Universal Music Group as “a pathway for fans to connect with the artists they love as well as to move fluidly from discovery to enjoyment of new music — and all within a monetised ecosystem”, the deal pointed to a wider trend. AI-driven conversational interfaces may be the next big shakeup for the way people find, play and share music. See also Amazon’s Alexa+ which launched this year with some smarter music features.
There were some negative music headlines for ChatGPT this year too, however. German collecting society GEMA
scored a win in a court battle in its homeland
over whether OpenAI infringed copyright by training its models on song lyrics. Similar issues are at the heart of
the ongoing lawsuit from a group of music publishers against Anthropic
over its Claude service.
D is for Deezer
Deezer isn’t one of the big players in global music-streaming: it had
nine million subscribers at the end of September
compared to Spotify’s 281 million. But Deezer has shown leadership in another way in 2025: around what it has said and done in relation to GenAI music.
The ‘said’ part of that has involved regularly announcing how much of the music being uploaded to its platform is fully AI-generated. The proportion rose from
10% in January
to
18% in April
,
28% in September
and then 34% in November. In terms of scale, that’s a rise from 10,000 daily GenAI tracks  in January to 50,000 by November.
Other stats shared by Deezer include the fact that fully AI-generated tracks only account for 0.5% of its total streams, and that
as many as 70% of those AI streams are fraudulent
. The company has thus shone a spotlight not just on an explosion of AI music being uploaded, but also on the fact that right now, only a tiny number of humans are actually choosing to listen to it. It’s valuable data.
Deezer also deserves praise for setting out some clear policies on how it treats GenAI music.
It tags it – based on its in-house detection technology
– so that listeners know when a track is GenAI, and it also removes these tracks from its algorithmic recommendations and editorial playlists.
E is for ElevenLabs
Until this year, we mainly knew tech firm ElevenLabs for its work around AI voice technology, building fully-licensed clones of dead celebrities (
including The Grateful Dead’s Jerry Garcia
) that could be used in its Reader app. However, 2025 is the year it expanded into GenAI music, initially via
a licensing deal with sync platform SourceAudio in July
.
The big news came the following month as the company
announced a new AI-music tool called Eleven Music
. Capable of generating “studio-quality tracks” in a matter of seconds, the initial version was trained on production music. However, ElevenLabs revealed deals with publisher Kobalt and indie licensing agency Merlin that would drive a next-generation ‘Eleven Music Pro’ model too.
Here’s why these deals are so interesting: they reportedly grant parity to recordings and compositions. That means any royalties from Eleven Music Pro will be split equally between publishers (and thus songwriters) and recordings rightsholders (and thus artists). It’s a break from past deals in the streaming space, which saw publishers take a lower share of royalties.
It’s unclear whether ElevenLabs’ approach will catch on for other GenAI-music services, even though
publishers would love it to
. Major labels have yet to announce their own deals with ElevenLabs, so their views on its parity approach remain unclear too.
F is for Fair Use
We could probably write an entire A-to-Z article on legal terms around the topic of ‘fair use’ – although to be honest, we’re not sure anyone would want to read it. Not even lawyers!
Fair use is a legal doctrine in the US that allows limited use of copyrighted material without needing permission or a licence for certain things – ranging from criticism and commentary to teaching and research – IF that use is considered to be reasonable and transformative.
That’s a simple-enough definition that masks a horde of complexities and arguments around what should and shouldn’t qualify for fair use. The relevant question here is whether GenAI music-creation tools should.
The major labels and collecting societies who sued companies like Suno and Udio said they absolutely should not. Those companies – and a number of other AI firms and their venture-capital backers – have argued that they should.
The wrinkle here is that while both sides of this debate firmly believe that they are right, neither can be sure if the decisions in any court cases will go their way. The risks of that are big, too: potentially-huge damages bills if the AI firms lose, and the entire history of recorded music being fair game for unlicensed training if the music industry loses.
This, we think, is a strong incentive for deals to be signed and lawsuits to be settled, which is what’s been happening in the final months of 2025. Udio settled and signed
with Universal Music Group
and
with Warner Music Group
, while Suno
settled and signed with WMG too
.
There have been some court judgements on fair use: for example
a pair of rulings in June in lawsuits against Anthropic and Meta
from groups of book authors. Both were initially hailed as wins for the tech companies, but as we explained in our analysis, there were
lots
of nuances to the rulings.
In summary: it hasn’t yet been proven decisively in court whether AIs being trained on copyrighted content (music included) is fair use or not, and there are persuasive reasons for rightsholders and AI firms alike to settle out of court to avoid a ruling that goes against them.
You can read part two of our A-to-Z of AI music in 2025 feature
here
.
Disclosure: some of the images in this article were created using Google’s Nano Banana AI: specifically the lead image plus the pics for the A, C and F entries.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
